title: Excuse me...
question_text: To politely get someone's attention or apologize
opt2: I'm lost. Can you help me?
explanation: Use "excuse me" before asking a stranger for help. \br \br After bumping into someone, say "excuse me" to apologize.
example1: Excuse me
example2: where is the nearest station?
example3: Excuse me, could you repeat that?

title: Where is the...?
question_text: A phrase to ask for a location
opt2: Where is the restroom?
explanation: Use this structure: "Where is the + [place]?" \br \br Common places: bank, supermarket, pharmacy, station, hotel.
example1: Where is the nearest ATM?
example2: Where is the reception desk?
usage1: Always say "excuse me" before this question.

title: How do I get to...?
question_text: A phrase to ask for directions to a place
opt2: How do I get to the city center?
explanation: Use for longer distances or when you need step-by-step directions. \br \br Answer might include: turn left, go straight, take the bus.
example1: How do I get to the airport?
example2: How do I get to the museum from here?
usage1: You can also say: "What's the best way to get to...?"

title: Go straight
question_text: Continue moving forward without turning
opt2: Go straight for two blocks.
explanation: Use when giving directions. \br \br "Block" = the area between two streets.
example1: Go straight until you see the traffic light.
example2: Go straight, then turn right.

title: Turn left / Turn right
question_text: Change direction to the left or right side
opt2: Turn left at the pharmacy.
explanation: Use when giving turns. \br \br "At the corner" is common: Turn right at the corner.
example1: Turn right after the bridge.
example2: Turn left, then go straight.

title: It's next to / It's between
question_text: Prepositions to describe where something is located
opt2: The bank is next to the supermarket.
explanation: "Next to" = beside. \br \br "Between" = with something on both sides.
example1: The pharmacy is between the bakery and the bank.
example2: My hotel is next to the station.
usage1: Also useful: across from, near, behind, in front of

title: Asking for Directions - Quiz 1
definition: You are lost. What do you say to start asking for help?
opt2: I'm lost.
example1: "Excuse me, I'm lost" is the polite and natural way. The other options are either impolite or grammatically weird.
example2: Excuse me, I'm lost. Can you help me find this address?

title: Directions - Fill the blank
definition: Complete the sentence with ONE word: "Go _______ for two blocks, then turn left."
sentence: go straight ______ for two blocks then turn left
opt2: right
explanation: The most common answer is "straight". "Straight ahead" also works but is two words.
example1: Go straight, then turn right at the bank.

title: Giving Directions Formula
definition: Structure for giving clear directions \br regular \br [direction verb] + [preposition] + [landmark]
question_text: Go straight until you see the church. \br Turn left at the traffic light. \br It's next to the post office.
sentence: Go straight for two blocks. \br Turn right at the corner. \br It's between the bank and the café.
opt2: Use landmarks (visible places) not street names when possible. \br \br Present tense is normal for directions.

title: At the Airport - Check-in
definition: What do you say at the check-in counter?
opt1: Here is my passport and boarding pass.
opt2: I want to fly.
explanation: "Here is my passport and boarding pass" is what you actually do/say. The others are too aggressive or incomplete.
example1: Here is my passport. I have one bag to check.

title: Boarding pass
question_text: The document that allows you to board the plane
opt2: May I see your boarding pass
example1: You get this after checking in. It has your seat number, gate, and boarding time. \br \br Don't lose it!
example2: I printed my boarding pass at home.
example3: The gate agent scanned my boarding pass.
usage1: Keep your boarding pass with your passport.

title: Gate
question_text: The area where you wait to board the plane
opt2: What gate is our flight?
explanation: The gate number is on your boarding pass. \br \br "Gate 12" or "Gate B7". \br \br Boarding starts about 30-45 minutes before departure.
example1: Our flight leaves from gate 15.
example2: They announced a gate change to gate 22.
usage1: Listen for announcements: "Flight BA247 is now boarding at gate 8."

title: Carry-on / Hand luggage
question_text: Small bag you take inside the plane
opt2: How many carry-on bags am I allowed?
explanation: Carry-on goes in the overhead bin. \br \br Personal item (purse, laptop bag) goes under the seat.
example1: My carry-on is too big for the bin.
example2: I only have carry-on, no checked bags.
usage1: Liquids in carry-on must be under 100ml.

title: Check-in luggage / Checked bag
question_text: Luggage you give to the airline at check-in
opt2: I have two bags to check.
example1: Checked bags go in the cargo hold. You pick them up at baggage claim after landing. \br \br Often there's a fee for checked bags.
example2: How much does it cost to check a bag?
example3: My checked bag was overweight, so I had to pay.

title: Take off (verb)
question_text: When the plane leaves the ground
opt2: What time do we take off?
explanation: Opposite = land. \br \br You must turn off electronics during takeoff and landing.
example1: The plane took off an hour late.
example2: Fasten your seatbelt for takeoff.

title: At the Airport - Fill the blank
definition: Complete the sentence: "After landing, you pick up your checked bags at _______ _______."
sentence: after landing you pick up your checked bags at ______ ______
opt2: cargo area
explanation: In American English it's "baggage claim". In British English it's "baggage reclaim".

title: At the Airport - Security
definition: What do you do at airport security?
opt1: Take off your shoes and belt.
opt2: Order a coffee.
explanation: You remove shoes, belts, jackets, and put liquids in a plastic bag. Electronics come out of your bag.
example1: You put your bags in a bin and send them through the X-ray machine.

title: Airport Sequence (Present Simple)
definition: The typical order of airport steps \br regular \br [step 1] + then + [step 2]
question_text: First check in, then go through security. \br After security, find your gate. \br Board the plane when they call your group.
sentence: Check in → get boarding pass → drop bags. \br Go to security → take off shoes → put bags on belt. \br Find gate → wait for boarding → show passport.
opt2: Use time words: first, then, after that, finally.

title: Boarding the plane
definition: The process of entering the plane before departure
question_text: They usually board by groups or rows. \br \br "Group 1 now boarding" means only group 1 can get on.
sentence: Your boarding pass will say "Group 3" or "Zone B". \br Listen for your group number.
opt1: "Now boarding rows 20 through 30." \br \br After boarding, find your seat and put bags away.
opt2: The flight attendant will say "Welcome aboard."

title: Grocery shopping - Aisle
question_text: A passage between shelves in a supermarket
opt2: Which aisle is the bread in?
explanation: Aisles have numbers. \br \br Common aisles: dairy, produce, bakery, frozen foods, canned goods.
example1: You can find cereal in aisle 5.
example2: The pasta aisle is next to the sauces.

title: Produce
question_text: Fresh fruits and vegetables
opt2: Where is the produce section?
explanation: Produce is usually the first section in many supermarkets. \br \br It's pronounced "PRO-duce" (not "pro-DUCE").
example1: The produce here is very fresh.
example2: I need to buy some produce: apples, bananas, and lettuce.

title: Dairy
question_text: Milk
opt2: butter
explanation: I need to go to the dairy aisle.
tip: Dairy products come from cows (or goats/soy/almond alternatives). \br \br Usually refrigerated.
column20: The dairy section is near the back of the store.
column21: Get milk, yogurt, and cheese from dairy.
column23: Some people are lactose intolerant (can't eat dairy).

title: Expiration date
question_text: The last day a product is safe to eat
opt2: Check the expiration date before buying.
example1: Also called "best by" or "use by" date. \br \br Don't buy if it's past the date.
example2: The milk expires tomorrow.
example3: These eggs have an expiration date of May 15th.
tip: You can still eat some things after the date if they look/smell fine.

title: Grocery Shopping - Fill blank
definition: Complete the sentence: "The price of this item isn't on the shelf. I need to _______ it at the scanner."
sentence: i need to ______ it at the scanner
opt2: check
explanation: You "scan" a product (pass it over the scanner) to see the price.
example1: Let me scan this to see how much it costs.

title: At the Grocery Store
definition: What do you do at the checkout?
opt2: You put items back on shelves.
explanation: First you put items on the belt. The cashier scans them. Then you pay by cash or card. Then you bag your groceries.
example1: Do you need any bags? Paper or plastic?

title: Shopping list
question_text: A written list of things you need to buy
opt2: I forgot my shopping list at home.
example1: Make a list before you go to save time and avoid impulse buys. \br \br Organize by aisle for efficiency.
example2: Let me check my shopping list.
tip: Digital lists on phone work too.

title: Shopping cart / Trolley
question_text: A wheeled basket for carrying groceries
opt2: Where can I find a shopping cart?
explanation: US = shopping cart. UK = trolley. \br \br Sometimes you need a coin to unlock one (you get the coin back).
example1: Grab a cart at the entrance.
example2: My cart has a wobbly wheel.

title: Comparing Prices (Superlatives)
definition: Structure for saying which product costs the most/least \br regular \br the + most/least + adjective
question_text: Brand A is the cheapest. \br Brand B is the most expensive. \br This store has the freshest produce.
sentence: Store A is the cheapest, but Store B has the best quality. \br Which milk is the least expensive?
opt2: Use "than" for comparisons: Store A is cheaper than Store B.

title: Restaurant - Ordering
definition: What do you say to the waiter when you're ready to order?
opt2: Give me food now.
explanation: "I'm ready to order" is polite and clear. The others are rude or ungrammatical.
example1: I'd like the steak, please. Medium rare.

title: Appetizer / Starter
question_text: Small dish eaten before the main meal
opt2: We shared an appetizer before dinner.
example1: US = appetizer. UK = starter. \br \br Examples: soup, salad, bread, spring rolls.
example2: I'll have the soup as a starter.
example3: The appetizer was delicious, but small.

title: Main course / Entrée
question_text: The largest or most important dish of a meal
opt2: What's your main course today?
explanation: US: entrée = main course. \br \br UK: entrée = starter (confusing!) \br \br Best to say "main course" everywhere to avoid confusion.
example1: For my main course
example2: I'll have the fish.
example3: The main course took 30 minutes to arrive.

title: Check / Bill
question_text: The paper showing what you owe after a meal
opt2: Can I have the check
example1: US = check. UK = bill. \br \br Often you need to ask for it (they don't bring it automatically in some countries).
example2: The check comes to $35 total.
example3: Let me pay the bill. I'll cover it.

title: Restaurant - Fill blank
definition: Complete what the waiter asks: "How would you like your steak _______?"
sentence: how would you like your steak ______
opt2: done
explanation: "Cooked" or "done" are both common. Answer: rare, medium rare, medium, medium well, or well done.
example1: I'd like it medium rare, please.

title: Restaurant - Complaints
definition: Your food is cold. What do you say to the waiter?
opt2: this is cold. Could you warm it please?
example1: Politely explain the problem and ask for a solution. "Excuse me" softens the complaint.
example2: Excuse me, I ordered the chicken but this is fish.

title: To-go / Takeout
question_text: Food you take away to eat somewhere else
opt2: I'd like this to go
example2: US = to-go / takeout. UK = takeaway. \br \br Opposite = for here (eat at restaurant).
tip: We got takeout and ate at the park.
column21: Some restaurants charge extra for takeout containers.

title: Polite Restaurant Requests
definition: Structure for asking nicely \br regular \br Could I have + [item] + please?
question_text: Could I have a glass of water, please? \br Could we get more napkins? \br I'd like the pasta, please.
sentence: Could I have the check? \br I'd like a table for two, please. \br Could we sit by the window?
opt2: Other polite phrases: May I...?, Is it possible to...?

title: Table setting
definition: The arrangement of plates
question_text: glasses
opt1: A basic place setting has: plate in center, fork on left, knife and spoon on right, glass above knife.
opt2: Fork on left, knife on right. Knife blade faces the plate.
opt3: The spoon is to the right of the knife. The napkin is on the plate or left of the forks.
opt4: Bread plate is top-left. Water glass is top-right.

title: Hotel - Check-in
question_text: Arriving and registering at a hotel
opt2: I have a reservation under the name Smith.
example1: You show your ID and credit card at check-in. \br \br You get your room key and room number.
example2: What time is check-in?
column20: Late check-in: call the hotel to say you'll arrive after 10 PM.

title: Reservation
question_text: A booking for a room or table
opt2: Do you have a reservation?
explanation: You make a reservation online or by phone. \br \br "Under the name of..." means the name used to book.
example1: I need to cancel my reservation.
example2: I'd like to make a reservation for two nights.

title: Hotel - Fill blank
definition: Complete the request: "Could I have a _______ key for room 412, please?"
sentence: could i have a ______ key for room 412 please
opt2: spare
explanation: "Spare key" or "extra key" are both correct. You say this if your partner has the other key.
example1: My wife has the other key. Could I get a spare?

title: Hotel - Housekeeping
definition: What does "housekeeping" mean?
opt1: The staff who clean hotel rooms.
opt2: The front desk.
explanation: Housekeeping is the cleaning department. You can call them for extra towels, soap, or to clean your room.
example1: Could I get some extra towels from housekeeping? I'm in room 412.

title: Wake-up call
question_text: A phone call from the front desk to wake you up
opt2: Can I set a wake-up call for 6:30 AM?
explanation: Old hotels still do this manually. Most hotels have an automated system now. \br \br Useful for early flights.
example1: I requested a wake-up call but nobody called.
example2: My wake-up call came 10 minutes late.
usage1: Set your phone alarm as a backup.

title: Hotel Requests (polite questions)
definition: Asking hotel staff for things \br regular \br Could I + [verb] + please?
question_text: Could I get extra pillows? \br Could you send someone to fix the TV? \br Is it possible to have a late check-out?
sentence: Could I have a room with a view? \br Could we get a rollaway bed? \br Is breakfast included?
opt2: Always say please and thank you. The front desk can help with almost anything.

title: Doctor - Symptoms
question_text: Physical feelings that indicate illness
opt2: What are your symptoms?
explanation: Common symptoms: fever, cough, headache, sore throat, runny nose, nausea, rash, pain. \br \br "I have a + [symptom]" : I have a headache. \br "I feel + [adjective]": I feel dizzy.
example1: I've had a fever for three days.
example2: My symptoms include coughing and a sore throat.
usage1: Call your doctor if symptoms get worse.

title: Doctor - Fill blank
definition: Complete: "I need to make an _______ to see the doctor."
sentence: i need to make an ______ to see the doctor
opt2: meeting
explanation: You "make an appointment" (schedule a time) to see a doctor. You can call or book online.
example1: I'd like to make an appointment for tomorrow morning, please.

title: At the Pharmacy
definition: What do you say to get medicine?
opt1: Do you have something for a headache?
opt2: Give me medicine.
explanation: Asking politely and describing your symptom (headache) is best. Pharmacists can recommend over-the-counter medicine.
example1: Do I need a prescription for this, or is it over-the-counter?

title: Prescription
question_text: A doctor's written order for medicine
opt2: I need to pick up my prescription.
example1: You take the prescription to a pharmacy. \br \br "Over-the-counter" (OTC) means no prescription needed.
example2: The doctor gave me a prescription for antibiotics.
tip: Refill = get more of the same medicine.

title: Phrases for pain description
definition: Telling a doctor where and how much it hurts
question_text: Use these phrases: \br "I have a sharp pain in my back." \br "It's a dull ache." \br "It hurts when I move."
sentence: "It's throbbing" = pain that pulses with heartbeat. \br "It's stabbing" = sudden sharp pain. \br "It's burning" = hot painful feeling.
opt1: Rate pain 1-10: "My pain is about a 7 out of 10." \br \br Point with your finger: "It hurts right here."
opt2: Use adjectives (sharp, dull, burning, throbbing) to be specific. Don't just say "it hurts" – explain how.

title: Bus stop / Bus station
question_text: Where you wait for a bus
opt2: Is there a bus stop near here?
explanation: Bus stop = small sign on street (local buses). Bus station = large building (long-distance buses).
example1: Which bus stop is for the number 42 bus?
example2: I'll wait for you at the bus station downtown.
usage1: Check the bus schedule (timetable) for times.

title: Public Transport - Fare
definition: What does "fare" mean?
opt2: The bus driver.
explanation: Fare = the money you pay to ride. You might pay cash, tap a card, or use a transit pass.
example1: What's the fare to the city center? $2.50.

title: Public Transport - Fill blank
definition: Complete: "Does this bus _______ the airport?"
opt2: stop at
explanation: Both "go to" and "stop at" are correct. You ask the driver or check the front sign.
example1: Does this train go to Grand Central Station?

title: Transfer
question_text: Changing from one bus/train to another
opt2: Do I need to transfer?
explanation: Direct = no transfer needed. \br \br Transfer ticket = allows you to switch for free (or cheap).
example1: You'll have to transfer at the next station.
example2: Is there a direct bus, or do I transfer?

title: Asing About Schedule (future simple)
definition: Using "will" and "does" for timetable questions \br regular \br What time + does + the [vehicle] + leave/arrive?
question_text: What time does the next train leave? \br When will the bus arrive? \br How often does this bus run?
sentence: What time does the last bus depart? \br Will we arrive on time? \br How frequently does the subway come?
opt2: Use present simple for fixed schedules: The train leaves at 8:15.

title: Office - Deadline
question_text: The date or time by which work must be finished
opt2: When is the deadline for this project?
explanation: Don't miss the deadline! \br \br "Meet a deadline" = finish on time. \br "Miss a deadline" = finish late.
example1: The deadline is Friday at 5 PM.
example2: Can we get an extension on the deadline?
usage1: "Deadline" is a single word, not "dead line".

title: Office - Fill blank
definition: Complete: "Could you please _______ me that document when you're done?"
sentence: could you please ______ me that document when you're done
opt2: send
explanation: All three work: send, email, or forward. "Forward" means send something someone else sent you.
example1: Could you forward me the meeting notes from yesterday?

title: Office - Meeting
definition: Your colleague is late. What do you say?
opt1: Shall we wait a few more minutes?
opt2: You are late.
explanation: "Shall we wait?'' is polite and suggests a solution. The others are accusatory or dramatic.
example1: I'll call her to see where she is. Shall we start without her?

title: On break
question_text: Taking time away from work to rest
opt2: I'm on my lunch break right now.
example1: Breaks are usually paid. Lunch break is usually unpaid. \br \br "Coffee break" = short 15-min break.
example2: Can I take my break at 2 PM instead?
example3: Sorry, I'm on break. I'll call you back in 20 minutes.
tip: Don't say "I'm in break" – it's always "on break".

title: Overtime
question_text: Time worked beyond normal hours
opt2: Do we get paid for overtime?
explanation: Often 1.5x or 2x normal pay (time and a half / double time). \br \br Some jobs have "unpaid overtime" (not great).
example1: I worked 10 hours of overtime last week.
example2: Overtime is mandatory this month – we're busy.
